sentences.text: Kasper geht allein durch den Wald.
sentences.translation: Kasper walks alone through the forest.

words.grammar: proper noun · subject of sentence
gehen · present tense · 3rd person singular
manner adverb · modifies geht
durch + accusative · always takes accusative case
der Wald · masculine · accusative after durch

sentences.text: Plötzlich hört er ein lautes Geräusch.
sentences.translation: Suddenly he hears a loud noise.

words.grammar: sentence-opening adverb · triggers verb-subject inversion: hört er
hören · present tense · 3rd person singular
das Geräusch · neuter · accusative · laut → lautes (strong adjective ending)

sentences.text: Ein Räuber steht hinter einem Baum.
sentences.translation: A robber is standing behind a tree.

words.grammar: der Räuber · masculine · nominative · subject
stehen · present tense · 3rd person singular
two-way preposition · dative here (location, not movement)
der Baum · masculine · dative after hinter (location)

sentences.text: Der Räuber ruft: Gib mir dein Geld!
sentences.translation: The robber shouts: Give me your money!

words.grammar: rufen · present tense · 3rd person singular · verb of speech introducing direct dialogue
geben · imperative · 2nd person singular · irregular: gibt → gib
ich · dative · indirect object
das Geld · neuter · accusative · dein = possessive pronoun (informal)

sentences.text: Kasper antwortet laut: Nein, das mache ich nicht!
sentences.translation: Kasper answers loudly: No, I won't do that!

words.grammar: antworten · present tense · 3rd person singular
manner adverb · same word as the adjective laut (loud)
nicht at end of clause negates the whole action · machen · present tense · 1st person singular

sentences.text: Kasper fragt die Kinder: Habt ihr den Räuber gesehen?
sentences.translation: Kasper asks the children: Did you see the robber?

words.grammar: fragen · present tense · 3rd person singular
das Kind · plural · accusative · direct object of fragt
haben · present perfect auxiliary · 2nd person plural · question inversion: verb before subject
sehen · past participle · used with haben to form Perfekt (conversational past)

sentences.text: Die Kinder rufen: Ja! Er ist dort drüben!
sentences.translation: The children shout: Yes! He is over there!

words.grammar: dort = there · drüben = on the other side · combined = over there (pointing away)
sein · present tense · 3rd person singular · er refers to der Räuber

sentences.text: Kasper läuft schnell hinter den Baum.
sentences.translation: Kasper runs quickly behind the tree.

words.grammar: laufen · present tense · 3rd person singular · vowel change: au → äu
manner adverb · same form as adjective schnell
hinter + accusative here (movement toward location) · contrast with s3: hinter einem Baum (dative, static)

sentences.text: Er packt den Räuber am Arm.
sentences.translation: He grabs the robber by the arm.

words.grammar: packen · present tense · 3rd person singular · strong physical action verb
der Räuber · masculine · accusative · direct object
an + dem → am (contraction) · dative · body part idiom: jemanden am Arm packen

sentences.text: Der Räuber ruft: Lass mich los! Lass mich los!
sentences.translation: The robber shouts: Let me go! Let me go!

words.grammar: lassen · imperative · 2nd person singular · irregular: lässt → lass
loslassen = separable verb (to let go) · mich = accusative of ich · repetition for dramatic effect

sentences.text: Aber Kasper hält ihn fest und ruft nach dem Polizisten.
sentences.translation: But Kasper holds him tight and calls for the policeman.

words.grammar: festhalten · separable verb · present tense · 3rd person singular · fest = tight, halten = to hold
er → ihn · masculine · accusative · refers to den Räuber
rufen nach + dative · to call for someone · nach here is not directional but idiomatic
der Polizist · masculine · dative after nach · weak noun: Polizist → Polizisten in all cases except nominative

sentences.text: Der Polizist kommt und nimmt den Räuber mit.
sentences.translation: The policeman comes and takes the robber away.

words.grammar: kommen · present tense · 3rd person singular
mitnehmen · separable verb · present tense · 3rd person singular · mit goes to end of clause
der Räuber · masculine · accusative · direct object of mitnehmen

grammarPoints.rule: Imperative (du-form) for commands → regular and irregular verbs
grammarPoints.explanation: To give a command to someone you address as 'du', use the verb stem without an ending. Most verbs are regular: 'kommen' → 'Komm!' But some are irregular: 'geben' → 'Gib!' (not 'Geb!') and 'lassen' → 'Lass!' (not 'Lass-e!'). These irregular forms must be memorised.

grammarPoints.rule: Negation with 'nicht' at the end of a clause
grammarPoints.explanation: When 'nicht' negates the whole action of a sentence, it goes to the very end of the clause. 'Das mache ich nicht' means 'I won't do that' → the 'nicht' cancels the entire verb action, not just one word.

grammarPoints.rule: 'Hinter' as a two-way preposition → dative for location, accusative for movement
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Ein Räuber steht hinter einem Baum. → Kasper läuft hinter den Baum.
grammarPoints.explanation: 'Hinter' (behind) can take two different cases depending on meaning. When describing where something IS (static location), use dative: 'hinter einem Baum' (standing behind a tree). When describing movement TOWARD a location, use accusative: 'hinter den Baum' (running behind the tree). The same rule applies to: an, auf, in, neben, über, unter, vor, zwischen.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Plötzlich hört er ein lautes Geräusch.
grammarPoints.explanation: In German, the verb must always be the second element in a statement. When a sentence begins with an adverb like 'plötzlich' (suddenly), the verb comes next → before the subject. So instead of 'Er hört plötzlich...', the word order flips to 'Plötzlich hört er...'. This is called inversion and happens whenever anything other than the subject opens the sentence.

grammarPoints.rule: Separable verbs → the prefix moves to the end of the clause
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Der Polizist nimmt den Räuber mit.
grammarPoints.explanation: Some German verbs are made of two parts: a base verb and a prefix that carries extra meaning. 'Mitnehmen' (to take along) splits into 'nimmt' (conjugated verb, position 2) and 'mit' (prefix, sent to the end). The same happens with 'festhalten' → 'hält...fest' and 'loslassen' → 'lass...los'. The prefix always travels to the very end of its clause.

grammarPoints.rule: Perfekt tense for spoken past → haben + past participle
grammarPoints.explanation: In spoken German, past events are expressed using the Perfekt: the auxiliary verb 'haben' (conjugated) plus the past participle at the end. 'Gesehen' is the past participle of 'sehen' (to see). In questions, 'haben' inverts before the subject: 'Habt ihr...' (Have you / Did you...). This is the tense learners will hear most in everyday conversation.

grammarPoints.rule: Verbs of speech introducing direct dialogue with a colon
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Der Räuber ruft: Gib mir dein Geld!
grammarPoints.explanation: When a narrator introduces what someone says, a verb of speech (rufen, sagen, fragen, antworten) is followed by a colon. The spoken words then follow exactly as they were said. This construction → 'X says/shouts/asks: [speech]' → is the core pattern of the hybrid dialogue format used throughout this story.
